flashcards.q: State the Law of Conservation of Mass.
flashcards.a: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. Total mass of reactants = total mass of products.

flashcards.q: State the Law of Constant Proportions.
flashcards.a: In a pure chemical compound, elements are always present in definite proportions by mass, regardless of source or method of preparation. Example: H:O in water = always 1:8.

flashcards.q: Which Dalton's postulate explains the Law of Conservation of Mass?
flashcards.a: Postulate (ii): Atoms are indivisible — they cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.

flashcards.q: Which Dalton's postulate explains the Law of Definite Proportions?
flashcards.a: Postulate (vi): The relative number and kinds of atoms in a given compound are constant.

flashcards.q: State Dalton's 6 postulates of atomic theory.
flashcards.a: (i) All matter = atoms. (ii) Atoms are indivisible, cannot be created/destroyed. (iii) Atoms of same element are identical. (iv) Atoms of different elements differ. (v) Atoms combine in small whole-number ratios. (vi) Kind and number of atoms in a compound is constant.

flashcards.q: Define atomic mass unit (u). What is the current standard?
flashcards.a: 1 u = 1/12th the mass of one carbon-12 atom (standard adopted in 1961). Previously called 'amu', now written as 'u' (unified mass unit).

flashcards.q: Why was carbon-12 chosen as the atomic mass standard (not oxygen)?
flashcards.a: Natural oxygen is a mixture of isotopes (O-16, O-17, O-18) causing inconsistent values. Carbon-12 is a single, specific, stable isotope giving reproducible, unambiguous results.

flashcards.q: What is the atomic radius of hydrogen? How is it expressed?
flashcards.a: ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ m. Atomic radius is measured in nanometres (nm); 1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m.

flashcards.q: Write the atomic masses of: H, C, N, O, Na, S, Cl, Ca.
Write the symbols and their Latin origins: iron, sodium, potassium, gold, silver, lead, copper.
flashcards.a: Fe (ferrum), Na (natrium), K (kalium), Au (aurum), Ag (argentum), Pb (plumbum), Cu (cuprum).

flashcards.q: IUPAC rules for writing element symbols?
flashcards.a: First letter: always UPPERCASE. Second letter (if any): always lowercase. Example: Aluminium → Al (not AL); Cobalt → Co (not CO).

flashcards.q: Why is Co different from CO?
flashcards.a: Co = cobalt (element, metal). CO = carbon monoxide (compound of C + O). Case matters — lowercase 'o' in Co avoids confusion with the compound CO.

flashcards.q: What is atomicity? Give examples for each type.
flashcards.a: Atomicity = number of atoms in one molecule. Monoatomic: He, Ar (noble gases). Diatomic: H₂, O₂, N₂, Cl₂, F₂. Triatomic: O₃. Tetraatomic: P₄. Polyatomic: S₈.

flashcards.q: Define a molecule.
flashcards.a: The smallest particle of an element or compound capable of independent existence that shows all properties of the substance. Made of 2 or more chemically bonded atoms.

flashcards.q: What is an ion? Distinguish cation from anion.
flashcards.a: Ion = charged species (atom/group with net charge). Cation (+): metal loses electrons (e.g., Na⁺, Ca²⁺). Anion (−): non-metal gains electrons (e.g., Cl⁻, O²⁻).

flashcards.q: Define a polyatomic ion. Give 4 examples.
flashcards.a: A group of atoms carrying a net charge acting as a single ion. Examples: OH⁻ (hydroxide), SO₄²⁻ (sulphate), NO₃⁻ (nitrate), NH₄⁺ (ammonium), CO₃²⁻ (carbonate), PO₄³⁻ (phosphate).

flashcards.q: What is valency? Give the valency of: H, O, N, Al, Ca, Na.
flashcards.a: Valency = combining power (capacity) of an element. H=1, O=2, N=3, Al=3, Ca=2, Na=1.

flashcards.q: Steps of the criss-cross method for writing formulae?
flashcards.a: 1. Write metal/cation first, non-metal/anion second. 2. Write valencies below. 3. Criss-cross valencies as subscripts. 4. Simplify (divide by GCD). 5. Use brackets for multiple polyatomic ions.

flashcards.q: Why is Ca(OH)₂ written with brackets and not CaOH₂?
flashcards.a: Two hydroxide (OH⁻) groups are present. Brackets with subscript outside show the whole OH group repeats twice. CaOH₂ would incorrectly suggest separate O and 2H atoms, not 2 complete OH units.

flashcards.q: Write formulae: MgCl₂, Al₂O₃, Na₂CO₃, Ca(OH)₂, (NH₄)₂SO₄.
flashcards.a: MgCl₂: Mg²⁺, Cl⁻. Al₂O₃: Al³⁺, O²⁻. Na₂CO₃: Na⁺, CO₃²⁻. Ca(OH)₂: Ca²⁺, OH⁻. (NH₄)₂SO₄: NH₄⁺, SO₄²⁻.

flashcards.q: Write formulae: Cu(NO₃)₂, AlCl₃, CaCO₃, NaNO₃.
flashcards.a: Cu(NO₃)₂: Cu²⁺, NO₃⁻. AlCl₃: Al³⁺, Cl⁻. CaCO₃: Ca²⁺, CO₃²⁻ (simplify Ca₂(CO₃)₂ → CaCO₃). NaNO₃: Na⁺, NO₃⁻.

flashcards.q: What does 'variable valency' mean? Give an example.
flashcards.a: Some elements can have more than one valency. Shown by Roman numerals in brackets. Example: Iron(II) = Fe²⁺ (ferrous), Iron(III) = Fe³⁺ (ferric). Copper(I) = Cu⁺, Copper(II) = Cu²⁺.

flashcards.q: Formula unit mass vs molecular mass — what is the difference?
flashcards.a: Molecular mass: used for covalent/molecular compounds (discrete molecules). Formula unit mass: used for ionic compounds (lattice of ions, no discrete molecules). Both calculated the same way — sum of atomic masses.

flashcards.q: Calculate molecular mass of H₂O, CO₂, NH₃, HNO₃.
Calculate formula unit mass of NaCl, CaCl₂, K₂CO₃.
Calculate molecular mass: C₂H₆, CH₄, Cl₂, C₂H₂ (ethyne).
How do you convert mass ratio to atom ratio?
flashcards.a: Divide the mass of each element by its atomic mass. Examples: H:O in water = 1/1 : 8/16 = 1:0.5 = 2:1 → H₂O. N:H in ammonia = 14/14 : 3/1 = 1:3 → NH₃.

flashcards.q: What is the Law of Multiple Proportions?
flashcards.a: When two elements form more than one compound, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other are in simple whole-number ratios. E.g., NO and NO₂: for same mass of N, O masses = 8 and 16 → ratio 1:2.

flashcards.q: Why can't most atoms exist independently?
flashcards.a: Most atoms have incomplete outermost electron shells — they are energetically unstable alone. They form bonds (covalent or ionic) to achieve stability. Exceptions: noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, etc.) have full shells and are inert.

flashcards.q: What is percentage composition? How is it calculated?
flashcards.a: Percentage by mass of each element in a compound. Formula: % of element = (mass of element in 1 molecule / molecular mass) × 100. Example: N in NH₃ = (14/17) × 100 = 82.35%.

flashcards.q: Name elements liquid at room temperature and their symbols.
flashcards.a: Mercury: Hg (metal). Bromine: Br₂ (non-metal). Both have melting points below 25°C.

flashcards.q: Write formulae and names: FeSO₄, Fe₂(SO₄)₃, FeCl₂, Fe₂O₃.
flashcards.a: FeSO₄: Iron(II) sulphate. Fe₂(SO₄)₃: Iron(III) sulphate. FeCl₂: Iron(II) chloride. Fe₂O₃: Iron(III) oxide.

flashcards.q: Formula unit mass of Al₂(SO₄)₃. (Al=27, S=32, O=16.)
Formula unit mass of (NH₄)₂SO₄. (N=14, H=1, S=32, O=16.)
What are the three types of matter particles? Which can exist independently?
flashcards.a: Atoms (usually cannot exist independently), Molecules (can exist independently — smallest unit showing all substance properties), Ions (exist in ionic compounds, charged). Noble gas atoms are the exception — monoatomic and independent.
